message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We previously reported that nasal SARS-CoV-2 viral loads (VL) peaked around the fourth day of symptoms in highly immune adults sampled April 2022 – April 2023, while influenza A VL peaked soon after symptom onset. We hypothesized that SARS-CoV-2 kinetics may have changed due to reduced COVID-19 incidence and altered vaccination patterns. Understanding how viral kinetics evolve over time is essential to inform testing strategies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants with symptomatic upper respiratory infection were recruited at testing centers in Georgia April 14, 2023-April 11, 2025. Participants reported date of symptom onset and vaccination history. A nasal swab was tested on the Xpert® Xpress CoV-2/Flu/RSV-Plus assay and Ct values recorded.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    552 adults (≥16 years) and 60 children (<16y) were SARS-CoV-2-positive; lowest median Ct values (highest VL) were observed on the 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    symptomatic day. Adults vaccinated within 12M (n=106) had peak VL on the 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    day, and those vaccinated ≥12M prior (n=334), on the 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    . In influenza A-positive adults (n=181) and children (n=147), median VL peaked on the 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    symptomatic day, versus the 4
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    day in 157 influenza B-positive participants.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In 2023-25, median SARS-CoV-2 VL peaked earlier relative to symptom onset compared to a highly immune 2022-23 cohort. More recent vaccination correlated with delayed peak VL, suggesting more robust immunity correlates with earlier symptom onset relative to peak. Median influenza A VL were highest at symptom onset, versus 4 days into symptoms for influenza B. These findings can inform use of multiplexed antigen tests amidst changing immunity and viral circulation patterns.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Summary Point</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In 2023-25, median nasal SARS-CoV-2 viral loads peaked around the second day of symptoms, versus the fourth day in a 2022-23 highly immune cohort. Median influenza A viral loads peaked soon after symptom onset, versus on the fourth symptomatic day for influenza B. These observations have implications for multiplexed testing.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Peak Nasal SARS-CoV-2 and Influenza Viral Loads Relative to Symptom Onset, 2022-2025: Impact of Vaccination and Implications for Multiplexed Testing
